Idiomatic Expressions

  • 客之术神矣 - The guest's skill is divine, meaning the guest's ability is extraordinary or miraculous.
  • 不受礼物则可 - It is acceptable not to accept gifts, indicating that the guest's refusal of gifts is understandable or justified.
  • 何至避一跪而水遁 - Why go so far as to avoid a bow and escape by water? This phrase suggests that the guest's behavior is overly extreme or unnecessary.
  • 可谓玄之又玄，众妙之门 - This can be called the most profound and mysterious, the gateway to all wonders, emphasizing the depth and complexity of the guest's actions or character.
  • 吾乡李生应童子试 - My fellow townsman, Mr. Li, took the examination for young scholars, referring to a traditional Chinese examination system.
  • 年至不惑，不得入泮 - At the age of forty, he did not enter the school, meaning he failed to pass the examination and enter the school.
  • 遂以训蒙为业 - He then took up teaching as a profession, indicating a change in his career.
  • 乡农延之，岁入修廿余缗 - The villagers hired him, and he earned more than twenty strings of cash annually, describing his income.
  • 及门者十数顽童，甘厌其烦，然舍此更无别业可为，不得不下气降心以就之，非其本心也 - A dozen or so unruly children came to his door, and though he found them tiresome, he had no other profession to fall back on, so he had to humble himself and do it, though it was not his true desire, highlighting his reluctant acceptance of the job.
  • 居停十余家，轮流供膳，有余则以施丐 - More than ten families took turns providing him with meals, and any surplus was given to beggars, describing the communal support he received.
  • 有一老人，日向先生求乞，不以为厌，求必饱而后去 - An old man came to the teacher every day to beg, and the teacher did not find him annoying; he would give until the old man was full before leaving, emphasizing the teacher's generosity.
  • 先生之疾，易为也 - The teacher's illness is easy to cure, suggesting that the illness is not serious.
  • 我往觅草药，煮而洗之，不日愈矣 - I will go and gather some herbs, boil them, and wash them on you, and you will recover soon, describing the treatment.
  • 未几，果以药草来，嗅之香甚 - In a short while, he indeed brought some herbs, which smelled very fragrant, describing the arrival of the herbs.
  • 如法熬煎洗，果就痊 - Following the method to boil and wash, he indeed recovered, indicating the success of the treatment.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 童子试 - Refers to the traditional Chinese examination for young scholars, a crucial step in the imperial examination system.
  • 不惑 - Refers to the age of forty, as described in the *Analects of Confucius*, where Confucius said, 'At forty, I no longer felt perplexed.'
  • 入泮 - Refers to entering a school or academy, specifically the local school where scholars studied.
  • 训蒙 - Refers to teaching children the basics of reading, writing, and arithmetic, a common profession in traditional Chinese society.
  • 乡农延之 - Refers to the practice of hiring teachers in rural areas, where education was often community-supported.
  • 居停 - Refers to the practice of taking turns to host or support a teacher, a communal responsibility in rural China.
  • 施丐 - Refers to the practice of giving surplus food or resources to beggars, reflecting the Confucian value of charity.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 客之术神矣，不受礼物则可，何至避一跪而水遁 - This sentence contrasts the guest's extraordinary skill with his extreme behavior, emphasizing the inconsistency between his abilities and actions.
  • 可谓玄之又玄，众妙之门 - This phrase is a metaphorical statement, comparing the guest's actions to the profound and mysterious nature of the Dao, as described in the *Dao De Jing*.
  • 吾乡李生应童子试，年至不惑，不得入泮，遂以训蒙为业 - This sentence describes Mr. Li's career path, from taking the examination to failing and then becoming a teacher.
  • 乡农延之，岁入修廿余缗 - This sentence describes Mr. Li's income, emphasizing the economic aspect of his profession.
  • 及门者十数顽童，甘厌其烦，然舍此更无别业可为，不得不下气降心以就之，非其本心也 - This sentence describes Mr. Li's reluctant acceptance of his job, highlighting his dissatisfaction.
  • 居停十余家，轮流供膳，有余则以施丐 - This sentence describes the communal support Mr. Li received, emphasizing the rural community's generosity.
  • 有一老人，日向先生求乞，不以为厌，求必饱而后去 - This sentence describes the teacher's generosity towards the old man, emphasizing his kindness.
  • 先生之疾，易为也 - This sentence describes the teacher's illness, suggesting that it is not serious.
  • 我往觅草药，煮而洗之，不日愈矣 - This sentence describes the treatment, emphasizing its simplicity and effectiveness.
  • 未几，果以药草来，嗅之香甚 - This sentence describes the arrival of the herbs, emphasizing their quality.
  • 如法熬煎洗，果就痊 - This sentence describes the successful treatment, emphasizing its effectiveness.

  • 李蒙师 - A proper noun, likely the name of the teacher or a character in the text.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 制钱 - Refers to *zhì qián* (制钱), a type of copper coin minted by the government in imperial China.
  • 博场 - A gambling house or betting place in classical Chinese.
  • 压宝 - A gambling term meaning 'to place a bet' or 'to gamble.'
  • 探囊而得 - A metaphor from the idiom *tàn náng ér dé* (探囊而得), meaning to take something from one's pocket, symbolizing effortless success.
  • 童蒙 - Refers to children who are being taught, or 'the naive and uninitiated,' often used in the context of teaching or education.
  • 福德 - A term from Buddhist and Daoist thought, meaning 'merit and virtue.'
  • 鬼神之忌 - 'The resentment of ghosts and gods.' In classical Chinese thought, supernatural beings could be angered by human greed or moral transgressions.
  • 仙人 - 'Immortals' or 'sages,' often used metaphorically for those who live a life of contentment and spiritual fulfillment.
  • 戒之！戒之！ - A classical exhortation meaning 'Be warned!' or 'Take heed!'

Proper Nouns

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 吾鄉唐生業儒 - My fellow townsman, Tang Sheng, pursued the Confucian path.
  • 應童子試 - To take the examination for boys (children). Refers to the *tongzi shi* (童子試), an early examination for young candidates.
  • 年三十不得游庠 - At thirty, he had not yet entered the school (庠). *游庠* is a metaphor for entering the local school or obtaining a degree.
  • 市侩 - A term for a merchant or tradesman, often with a slightly derogatory tone, implying someone focused on profit rather than scholarship.
  • 不得赡养乃翁 - Cannot support your father. *乃翁* is a respectful term for 'your father' or 'your father-in-law.'
  • 无已 - A classical expression meaning 'if there is no other way' or 'if there is no alternative.'
  • 曷往依之 - 'Why not go and rely on him?' *曷* is a classical particle meaning 'why not' or 'why.'
  • 博升斗以养家 - To earn a small amount (升斗) to support the family. *升斗* is a measure of grain, used metaphorically for a modest income.
  • 坐食 - To live off others or to be idle and eat without working.
  • 诺诺 - A term indicating hesitation or reluctance, often used to describe someone who agrees but is not confident.
  • 自惭不能博一衿 - He felt ashamed that he could not obtain a *jin* (a scholar's robe). *博一衿* is a metaphor for obtaining a degree or passing an exam.
  • 改业 - To change one’s profession or to switch careers.
  • 随粮艘北上 - To travel north by grain ships. *粮艘* refers to ships used to transport grain.
  • 立南货肆 - Set up a southern goods shop. *南货* refers to goods from the south, often including foodstuffs and luxury items.
  • 司帐 - To manage accounts or to be in charge of bookkeeping.
  • 岁得数十金 - Earn tens of gold coins each year. *金* is a unit of currency, often referring to silver or gold coins.
  • 寄家 - Send home or send to the family.
  • 小补 - A small help or a minor contribution.
  • 果勤能也 - If you are diligent and capable. *果* means 'if indeed' or 'if truly.'
  • 再益汝俸 - I will increase your salary. *俸* refers to salary or stipend.
  • 安之 - He accepted it or he was content with it.
  • 喜读书 - Loves reading or is fond of studying.
  • 手不释卷 - Never lets go of the book – a classical idiom for being devoted to reading.
  • 兴至则拍案朗诵 - When inspired, he would bang the table and recite aloud. *拍案* is a vivid image of emotional recitation.
  • 肆伙及买物者见之，皆大笑 - The shop assistants and customers saw this, and all laughed. *肆伙* refers to the shop staff or assistants.
  • 叔愠甚 - His uncle was greatly angered. *愠* means to be angry or resentful.
  • 屡诤不改 - Repeatedly argued but did not change. *诤* is to admonish or remonstrate.
  • 伴两弟读亦可 - You can accompany your two younger brothers in their studies. *伴* means to accompany or be with.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Idiomatic Expressions

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 理堂名敷正 - Classical naming structure: 'Li Tang, whose name is Fu Zheng.'
  • 随其叔之任丹徒 - Followed his uncle to his post in Dantu – a common phrase indicating accompanying a relative to a new official post.
  • 值英夷犯顺 - 'When the English barbarians violated the right order' – '英夷' is a derogatory term for Western foreigners, especially British, used in the Qing period; '犯顺' means 'to violate the right order' or 'to attack the virtuous.'
  • 中丞檄属员之能解围者 - 'The provincial governor issued a summons to subordinates who could relieve the siege' – '檄' is an official summons or edict.
  • 其叔招募乡勇 - 'His uncle recruited local volunteers' – '乡勇' refers to local militias or armed civilians.
  • 使理堂择得千余人 - 'He sent Li Tang to select over a thousand people' – '择得' is a classical verb meaning 'to select or choose.'
  • 是行也，有死之心，无生之路，方能奋发有为 - 'In this mission, one must have a resolve to die, not a path to survival, in order to be truly vigorous and effective.' – A classical expression of total commitment, often used in military or loyalist contexts.
  • 汝曹归与父兄谋，不愿者退，无相强也 - 'You all should go and consult your fathers and brothers; those who do not wish to go should withdraw, do not force them.' – '汝曹' is a classical plural form meaning 'you all.'
  • 父兄有训，我等所以应募者，急父母之急，非仅崇明也 - 'Our fathers and brothers have given us instructions; the reason we responded to the recruitment is that we are eager to relieve the urgency of our parents, not just for Chongming.' – A moral appeal to filial duty.
  • 官能视我等如子，焉有子而敢背亲命者乎 - 'If officials can regard us as their children, how could any child dare to disobey the command of their parents?' – A rhetorical question emphasizing loyalty and filial piety.
  • 皆愿死斗 - 'All were willing to fight to the death' – a strong expression of resolve.
  • 复查其孤子及兄弟同役者，退一人 - 'After reviewing those who were orphans or whose brothers were serving, one person was withdrawn' – '复查' means 'reviewed again' or 're-examined.'
  • 得敢死之士八百 - 'They obtained eight hundred brave warriors ready to die' – '敢死之士' is a classical term for 'dare-to-die warriors' or 'courageous soldiers.'
  • 欢呼听令，遂给粮饷器械而操演之 - 'They cheered and listened to the orders, and then were given provisions, weapons, and trained.' – '操演' is a classical term for military drills or training.
  • 与其叔航海而前 - 'They set sail with his uncle at the head' – '航海' is to sail by sea, and '而前' is a classical construction meaning 'leading the way.'
  • 时军门所发之救兵千人亦到 - 'At that time, a thousand soldiers dispatched by the commander also arrived' – '军门' is a title for a high-ranking military official or commander.
  • 见乡勇皆踊跃争先，兵气亦作 - 'They saw the local volunteers all rushing forward eagerly, and the soldiers' morale also rose.' – '兵气' refers to the spirit or morale of the soldiers.
  • 二十艘乘风破浪前进 - 'Twenty ships advanced, riding the wind and waves' – a classical metaphor for swift and determined movement.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 王理堂 - The subject of the passage, a historical figure. His full name is **王敷正 (Wang Fu Zheng)**, and he is known as **理堂** (Li Tang), a classical honorific or nickname.
  • 河南光山县 - A county in Henan Province, China.
  • 丹徒 - A county in Jiangsu Province, historically an important military and administrative center.
  • 英夷 - A derogatory term used in the Qing dynasty to refer to Western foreigners, especially the British. It reflects the xenophobic and Sinocentric worldview of the time.
  • 犯顺 - A classical term meaning 'to violate the right order' or 'to attack the virtuous,' often used in the context of war or rebellion.
  • 中丞 - A title for a provincial governor or high-ranking official, often used in the Qing period.
  • 檄 - A classical term for an official summons or military edict.
  • 乡勇 - Local militias or civilian volunteers organized for defense, common during the Qing dynasty, especially in the context of foreign invasions.
  • 父兄有训 - Refers to the traditional Confucian emphasis on filial piety and respect for elders.
  • 敢死之士 - A classical term for warriors who are willing to fight to the death, often used in military and loyalty contexts.
  • 军门 - A high-ranking military official or commander, often in charge of a military region or campaign.
  • 乘风破浪 - A classical idiom meaning to advance with determination and speed, often used in military or heroic contexts.

  • 扣一两平之说 - The deduction of one liang per level.
  • 宪司所发兵饷，扣与不扣尚无明文 - Military rations issued by the censorate, whether to deduct or not, still lack clear regulations.
  • 刺史请示而后敢给 - The governor requests approval before daring to distribute.
  • 至今尚未见批回 - Until now, no approval has been seen.
  • 能不激成大祸乎 - How could this not provoke a great disaster?
  • 公即谕令兵目往刺史署长跪请罪 - You should instruct the soldiers to go to the governor's office and kneel to ask for forgiveness.
  • 吾将劝刺史自垫，立时给散 - I will persuade the governor to advance the funds and distribute them immediately.
  • 一军皆悦曰 - The entire army was pleased and said.
  • 我曹非委员公恩，父母妻子皆将冻馁 - If not for the commissioner's grace, our parents, wives, and children would all freeze and starve.
  • 此恩此德可弗图报 - This grace and virtue cannot be left unrepaid.
  • 随公制匾额 - The commissioner then made a plaque.
  • 主阃率队伍戎装，鼓乐卤簿，送至元帝庙 - The commander led the troops in full military attire, with music and a grand procession, to the Temple of Emperor Yuan.
  • 群拜而悬之 - They all bowed and hung it.
  • 甲辰仲春，理堂事毕，奉调旋省 - In the second month of the year Jia Chen, after completing his duties, the governor was summoned back to the capital.
  • 以万民朱盖导进出境 - With red umbrellas held by the people guiding him in and out of the city.
  • 至今两匾悬元帝庙门，人皆仰之 - To this day, the two plaques hang at the entrance of the Temple of Emperor Yuan, and people all look up to them.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 刺史 - Governor or provincial administrator in ancient China.
  • 宪司 - Censorate, a government body responsible for oversight and administration.
  • 元帝庙 - Temple dedicated to Emperor Yuan, a historical figure.
  • 甲辰 - Specific year in the Chinese zodiac calendar, which helps to date the events.
  • 万民朱盖 - Traditional Chinese ceremonial practice where red umbrellas are used to honor and guide important figures.

Proper Nouns

  • 刺史 - Governor or provincial administrator.
  • 宪司 - Censorate.
  • 元帝庙 - Temple of Emperor Yuan.
  • 甲辰 - Year Jia Chen.
  • 万民朱盖 - Red umbrellas held by the people.

  • 微员末职 - Refers to minor officials or low-ranking positions.

  • 理堂展其才力 - Means 'the commissioner demonstrates his talents and abilities.'
  • 非知仁勇三者兼全不能 - Means 'only one who possesses all three virtues—wisdom, benevolence, and courage—can achieve this,' indicating that the commissioner must have all these qualities.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 委员 - Refers to officials or commissioners, a term used in ancient Chinese governance.
  • 匾额 - Refers to plaques or inscriptions hung in public places to honor individuals or institutions.
  • 知仁勇 - Refers to the three cardinal virtues of wisdom (知), benevolence (仁), and courage (勇), which are central to Confucian thought.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 吏役送卷 - Literally 'officers and clerks delivering scrolls,' referring to officials and clerks bringing documents for the magistrate's review.

  • 刑罚甚重 - Literally 'punishments are very severe,' describing the harshness of the legal penalties.

  • 哀嚎甚惨 - Literally 'cries of pain are very pitiful,' emphasizing the intensity of the suffering.

  • 第见一起毕，则卷案送呈 - Literally 'only seeing each case completed, then the scroll is presented,' describing the procedural aspect of the court proceedings.
  • 青衣吏接展案上，仅露年月 - Literally 'a blue-robed clerk receives and unfolds it on the desk, only showing the date,' indicating the clerk's role in handling the documents.
  • 请生以朱笔某日书行 - Literally 'asks the magistrate to write with red ink on a certain date,' describing the act of signing the document.

  • 忽一阵香风，吹往西方去矣 - Literally 'suddenly a fragrant wind blows the woman away to the west,' indicating a mystical departure.
  • 吏请生归座，以签票一卷送案上 - Literally 'the clerk asks the magistrate to return to his seat and presents a scroll of tickets on the desk,' describing the continuation of the court proceedings.
  • 仅露其姓名，请生过朱判行 - Literally 'only showing the names, asking the magistrate to sign and approve,' indicating the formal approval process.
  • 又呈女犯签票亦如前 - Literally 'then presents a scroll of tickets for female offenders as before,' indicating the repetition of the process for female offenders.
  • 生点至某氏，心忽动 - Literally 'the magistrate points to a certain surname, and his heart suddenly moves,' suggesting a moment of recognition or emotional response.
  • 疑似其姑母，时方有疾 - Literally 'suspects it is his aunt, who is currently ill,' indicating a personal connection and concern.
  • 欲再阅，吏即持去 - Literally 'wants to read again, but the clerk takes it away,' showing the abrupt end of the magistrate's attempt to review further details.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 吏役 - Refers to the officials and clerks who assist the magistrate in the courtroom.
  • 刑罚 - Refers to the legal punishments administered in ancient China, which could be extremely severe and often included physical mutilation.
  • 青衣吏 - Refers to clerks dressed in blue, a common color for official attire in ancient China.
  • 朱笔 - Refers to red ink, often used for important official documents and signatures.
  • 红云下垂，光华灿灿 - Describes a vision of a red cloud hanging down with brilliant light, which could symbolize a divine or supernatural presence.
  • 金童玉女 - Refers to golden boys and jade girls, often depicted in Chinese mythology as attendants of deities or spiritual figures.
  • 黄瘦中年妇人 - Describes a thin middle-aged woman, possibly a deity or a spiritual figure.
  • 香风 - Refers to a fragrant wind, often associated with divine or supernatural phenomena in Chinese literature.
  • 西方 - Refers to the west, which in Chinese culture is often associated with the afterlife or the realm of the dead.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This section highlights the syntactic ambiguities in the source text, such as the unclear sequence of events in '吏役送卷，自外带囚就讯' and the symbolic vision in '天际红云下垂，光华灿灿中，有金童玉女擎幡盖，侍一黄瘦中年妇人.'
